---
title: "Business Understanding"
author: "Your Name"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Purpose

<!--
What is this project trying to accomplish, and why does it matter?
Frame it like a real business problem, not a class assignment.
-->

## The Business Problem

<!--
What is Rossmann (or your retailer) actually trying to decide using
demand forecasts? E.g. inventory ordering, staffing levels, markdown
timing, replenishment schedules. Be specific about the decision, not
just "sales."
-->

## Who Uses This Forecast

<!--
Who in a real organization consumes a forecast like this, and what do
they do differently depending on what the forecast says? Think about
the different audiences: e.g. store-level ops vs. regional planning
vs. finance/exec reporting. Draw on what you've seen in your own job.
-->

## What "Good" Looks Like

<!--
Pick a real accuracy metric (MAPE, WAPE, bias, RMSE, etc.) and justify
why it fits this specific problem. Is bias more important than raw
error here? Does the metric need to be weighted by revenue or volume?
-->

## Cost of Being Wrong

<!--
Forecasting errors are usually asymmetric. What does it cost the
business to overforecast (excess inventory, markdowns, waste) versus
underforecast (stockouts, lost sales, reputational cost)? This section
is often missing from portfolio projects, so don't skip it.
-->

## Constraints and Assumptions

<!--
Any known limitations of this exercise vs. a real production
environment: no live data feed, historical data only, simplified
scope, etc. Naming these shows judgment.
-->

## Next Steps

<!--
Brief pointer to what comes next (data exploration) and what
questions you're carrying into it.
-->
